question.text: Suresh invests Rs.5,000 per month at 12% per annum from age 25 to age 35 (10 years) and then stops. He leaves the corpus invested until age 60. His friend Ramesh starts investing Rs.5,000 per month at age 35 and continues until age 60 (25 years) at the same rate. Who will have a larger corpus at age 60?

answer.text: Ramesh, because he invested for a longer period
Both will have approximately the same corpus
Suresh, because his money had more time to compound
Cannot be determined without knowing the exact rate

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Suresh's corpus at 35 is approximately Rs.11.6 lakh, which grows at 12% for 25 more years to about Rs.1.98 crore. Ramesh invests for 25 years and accumulates about Rs.1.90 crore. Suresh wins despite contributing only Rs.6 lakh vs Ramesh's Rs.15 lakh. **The Trap:** Option A is the intuitive but wrong answer — longer investment period does not compensate for lost compounding time. This is the classic early starter advantage. **Cross-Exam Context:** This exact comparison appears in the NISM XVII workbook. Similar examples are used in Series V-A and X-A to demonstrate SIP benefits. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This is the most powerful argument for starting early. Suresh invested Rs.6 lakh total vs Ramesh's Rs.15 lakh, yet Suresh ends up with more money.
